title: Authentication & Authorization
subtitle: Login flows, JWT, sessions vs tokens, and OAuth explained

sections.title: The Login Flow
sections.text:
Here's how login actually works:

  1. User sends POST /login with email + password.
  2. Server hashes the password and compares it to the stored hash.
  3. If it matches, the server creates a token or session that proves "this user is logged in."
  4. The client stores this credential and sends it with every request.
  5. Server verifies it on each protected request.

Authentication (AuthN) = "Who are you?" — proving identity. Authorization (AuthZ) = "What can you do?" — checking permissions. They're different things, and mixing them up is a classic mistake.

steps.label: Authenticated!
steps.desc: Client includes credential in every request

sections.caption: The five-step login flow: credentials in, credential out

sections.title: Password Hashing
sections.text:
Never store passwords in plain text. Ever. Hash them with an algorithm designed for passwords — bcrypt or argon2. These are intentionally slow (taking ~100ms per hash instead of microseconds) so brute-force attacks are impractical.

Each password gets a unique salt — random data mixed in before hashing. This means even if two users have the same password, their hashes look completely different. The salt is stored INSIDE the hash string, so you don't need a separate salt column.

Never use SHA-256 or MD5 for passwords. They're fast by design — an attacker can try billions of guesses per second with a GPU.
sections.caption: Password hashing: salt + slow hash function = secure storage
sections.callout: Use bcrypt with cost factor 12. Higher = slower = more secure. Argon2 is even better but less widely available. Never use MD5 or SHA-256 for passwords.

sections.title: Sessions vs JWT Tokens
sections.text:
Sessions — The server stores login state. The client just holds a cookie with a session ID. Easy to revoke (delete the session), but needs server-side storage (use Redis for multi-server setups). Traditional, secure, simple.

JWT (JSON Web Tokens) — All user info is encoded in a signed token the client holds. The server verifies the signature without any database lookup. Stateless and scalable. But harder to revoke (need a blocklist) and tokens can grow large.

Which one? JWTs are popular for APIs and mobile apps. Sessions are simpler for traditional web apps. Many production systems use both.

items: Client holds session ID cookie only
All data encoded in the token
Can grow large with many claims
Must verify signature on every request

sections.caption: Sessions are server-side and revocable; JWT is stateless and scalable
sections.detail: Many modern applications use both: JWT for API authentication (mobile apps, SPAs) and sessions for web apps where server-side revocation is important. If you have a single server, sessions are simpler. If you have many stateless microservices, JWT is more natural. OAuth 2.0 combines both — the authorization server issues tokens that resource servers verify.
